coredata.dc:title: A biosensor based on carbon dots-protein interactions for specific and sensitive detection of pepsin in saliva
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chenzhao
Jiang, Yuhao
Wu, Zhiyu
Wang, Xiujuan
Yang, Jiawei
Li, Junxian
Wei, Shanshan
Sun, Guoying
Wang, Zhibing
coredata.dc:description: Salivary pepsin has emerged as a promising biomarker for rapid screening and diagnosis of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Pepsin mainly exists in strongly acidic environments and exhibits its highest activity. However, the poor stability of most fluorescent sensors in strong acidic environments brings a significant challenge for pepsin detection. Herein, an innovative biosensor was developed for the highly specific and sensitive detection of pepsin on the basis of green-emitting ionic liquid-based carbon dots (G-IL-CDs) conjugated with whey proteins (WPs). The G-IL-CDs exhibited aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement when interacting with WP, and the fluorescence intensity decreased after incubation with pepsin due to the disruption of the aggregation structure. This strategy is highly selective for pepsin due to the strongly acidic environment in which other proteases are inactivated. Under optimal experimental conditions, this biosensor successfully detected pepsin in real human saliva with a satisfying recovery. Furthermore, this study not only developed a CDs-based sensor for detecting pepsin but also laid a solid theoretical foundation for the future development of novel biosensors combining CDs and proteins.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biosensor
Pepsin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HR-TEM images of G-IL-CDs. (B) particle size distribution and (C) XRD pattern of G-IL-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectra of ILs, MPD and G-IL-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the G-IL-CDs. Inset: pictures under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (B) Fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelength.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the “increase-decrease” detection mechanism. (B) Fluorescence spectra of WP after adding different concentrations of G-IL-CDs. (C) Fluorescence Lifetime of G-IL-CDs and G-IL-CDs + WP. (D) SV curves of G-IL-CDs on WP at three different temperatures (303 K, 313 K, and 323 K). (E) UV–vis Spectra of G-IL-CDs, WP, and G-IL-CDs + WP. (F) Circular dichroism of WP and WP + G-IL-CDs.
The effects of pH (A), WP amount (B), incubation time (C) and incubation temperature (D) on the ΔF of G-IL-CDs-WP system.
(A) Effect of different interfering substances on ΔF. (B) The relationship between ΔF and pepsin concentration [C].
Preparation of G-IL-CDs and their application in pepsin detection.

head.ce:title: A biosensor based on carbon dots-protein interactions for specific and sensitive detection of pepsin in saliva

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology,2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: G. Sun, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Salivary pepsin has emerged as a promising biomarker for rapid screening and diagnosis of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Pepsin mainly exists in strongly acidic environments and exhibits its highest activity. However, the poor stability of most fluorescent sensors in strong acidic environments brings a significant challenge for pepsin detection. Herein, an innovative biosensor was developed for the highly specific and sensitive detection of pepsin on the basis of green-emitting ionic liquid-based carbon dots (G-IL-CDs) conjugated with whey proteins (WPs). The G-IL-CDs exhibited aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement when interacting with WP, and the fluorescence intensity decreased after incubation with pepsin due to the disruption of the aggregation structure. This strategy is highly selective for pepsin due to the strongly acidic environment in which other proteases are inactivated. Under optimal experimental conditions, this biosensor successfully detected pepsin in real human saliva with a satisfying recovery. Furthermore, this study not only developed a CDs-based sensor for detecting pepsin but also laid a solid theoretical foundation for the future development of novel biosensors combining CDs and proteins.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: GERD is a prevalent gastrointestinal disorder, characterized by the escape of acidic gastric contents and digestive enzymes from the stomach [1]. Common symptoms of GERD include a burning sensation in the chest, acid reflux, throat pain, difficulty swallowing, coughing, and even asthma. If left untreated, GERD can lead to several serious complications such as esophagitis, esophageal stricture, and esophageal ulcers [2]. It is noteworthy that GERD symptoms vary due to individual differences, sometimes manifesting as unusual problems like dental erosion or a sensation of foreign objects in the mouth. This often leads to misdiagnosis and delays in crucial treatment [3]. Typically, diagnosing GERD relies on clinical evaluation by physicians, coupled with a patient's medical history and symptoms. Further examinations include endoscopy [4], esophageal manometry [5], and 24-h MII-pH dynamic monitoring [6] to conclusively confirm the diagnosis. However, these testing methods come with certain drawbacks, such as invasiveness (which could cause discomfort and pose certain risks to patients) and high costs (requiring specialized equipment and trained medical professionals). Therefore, the aim of this study is to develop a high-performance fluorescent probe and a novel fluorescent detection method for the rapid, highly sensitive, cost-effective, and non-invasive diagnosis of GERD.

Pepsin is a proteolytic enzyme primarily secreted by stomach cells and scarcely present in the saliva of normal individuals, but it exhibits a notably elevated concentration in patients suffering from GERD [7]. In comparison to traditional invasive examination methods, detecting pepsin in saliva represents a non-invasive approach that does not require device insertion or endoscopic procedures [8]. Saliva collection is relatively straightforward without undergoing complex processes, requiring patients only to provide a saliva sample [9]. Consequently, pepsin in saliva emerges as a promising biomarker for GERD, which is suitable for rapid screening and diagnosis of the condition [10,11].

A variety of methods have been developed so far for the detection of pepsin, and each method possesses unique characteristics and application advantages. Specifically, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) achieves high sensitivity and specificity in detecting pepsin by using the specific binding between antibodies and pepsin, but it requires a relatively longer experimental time [12]. Molecular imprinting utilizes polymers imprinted with pepsin molecules to identify and capture the enzyme, but its preparation process is intricate [13]. Electrochemical sensors detect the concentration of pepsin through measuring the rate of electrochemical reactions, but they require support from specialized instrumentation [14]. Meanwhile, fluorescence methods are widely adopted due to their rapidity, high sensitivity, and strong anti-interference capabilities [15]. Currently, there have been few reports on the use of fluorescence-based methods for detecting pepsin. Li et al. developed a fluorescence sensor based on lysozyme-stabilized Au nanoclusters for pepsin detection in aqueous solution [16]. Jiang et al. established a simple method using squaraine dye as fluorescent probe for pepsin determination in saliva [17]. However, these fluorescent materials (Au nanoclusters and organic fluorescent dyes) had issues with poor stability or high biological toxicity, which restricted their application in complex substrates. Therefore, the developing of pepsin fluorescence probes with low toxicity, high sensitivity, excellent optical properties, and table operation in complex matrices still poses significant challenges.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of carbon materials with sizes smaller than 10 nm and spherical structures [18,19]. They have garnered increasing attention due to their simple preparation, low cost, ease of modification, excellent hydrophilicity, outstanding fluorescence stability, and high quantum yield [20–22]. However, most CDs are unable to effectively detect targets in complex matrices. In order to achieve stable applications within complex biological systems, ionic liquid-based carbon dots (IL-CDs) have gradually become a research hotspot [23]. It is attributed to several outstanding properties of IL-CDs. Firstly, IL-CDs enabled precise alteration of their optical, electronic, and surface properties to meet diverse application demands. Secondly, IL-CDs exhibit high fluorescence quantum yield, which makes them powerful tools in areas such as bioimaging, cellular labeling, and molecular probes [24,25]. Finally, the surface of IL-CDs can be designed to possess specific chemical functional groups, which enable them to interact with particular molecules or substances [26]. Based on the advantages of IL-CDs, novel green-emitting ionic liquid-based carbon dots (G-IL-CDs) were developed for determining the concentration of pepsin in saliva (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, G-IL-CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using 1-methyl-3-octylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([OMIM]PF4) and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) as raw materials. G-IL-CDs exhibited aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement in the presence of whey protein (WP), and the aggregation structure could be disrupted after incubation with pepsin, leading to the decrease in fluorescence. The prepared G-IL-CDs could maintain excellent fluorescence stability even in a strong acidic environment, which endowed them with high selectivity towards pepsin. In comparison to other methods, this method is suitable for the complex environment of pepsin and does not require prior sample treatment. Furthermore, G-IL-CDs successfully detected pepsin in human saliva samples, providing a viable method for quickly screening GERD. In summary, this study provided strong support for the application of G-IL-CDs in the field of biomedical diagnostics, not only optimizing the detection method but also furnishing new tools for the early diagnosis of GERD.

The 1-methyl-3-octylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([OMIM]PF4) and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) were purchased from Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Zinc acetate, magnesium sulfate, aluminum nitrate, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, copper sulfate, and silver nitrate were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Lipase (Lip), amylase (Amy), trypsin (Try), and pepsin (Pep) were provided from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water was prepared using a laboratory pure water system of HHitech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemical reagents were acquired from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China).

The morphology of G-IL-CDs was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100F). The Fourier-transform infrared spectra of G-IL-CDs were recorded on an infrared spectrophotometer (Thermofisher, Nicolet IS50). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra ranging from 10° to 80° were recorded utilizing an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, SmartLab-9 kW). Elemental composition and valence data were collected using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Malvern Panalytical, X′Pert3 MRD). UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra were respectively obtained using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary50) and a fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, LS-55).

Initially, 0.50 g of [OMIM]PF4 and 0.25 g of MPD were dissolved into 20 mL of water in a 50 mL high-pressure reactor. Subsequently, the mixture was heated at 180 °C in an oven for 12 h. After the reaction vessel cooled to room temperature, a solution of deep green CDs was obtained. For further purification, the CDs solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, the solution was transferred into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da and dialyzed in deionized water at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the purified G-IL-CDs solution was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C (Fig. S1).

Firstly, G-IL-CDs and WP were separately dissolved in deionized water to prepare a G-IL-CDs solution with a concentration of 7.50 × 10−3 g/mL and a WP solution with a concentration of 0.10 g/mL. Pepsin and other potentially interfering proteins were also separately dissolved in deionized water. During the pepsin detection process, 10 μL of G-IL-CDs solution and 40 μL of WP solution were added to 2.95 mL of buffer solution at pH 2.0 to form the G-IL-CDs-WP fluorescence probe system. Subsequently, different concentrations of pepsin solution were added to the probe solution and incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. The spectral data of G-IL-CDs-WP before and after the incubation were recorded.

Various interference ions (Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+) and interfering enzymes (Lip, Amy, Try) were added to the G-IL-CDs-WP system at identical concentrations (0.1 mg/mL), and the spectral data of the G-IL-CDs-WP system were recorded after 20 min. Saliva samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant was collected and diluted 10-fold with buffer solution. The recovery experiments at three spiked levels (low, medium, and high) were conducted with triplicate measurements to determine the recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD).

A transient fluorescence analyzer was utilized to measure the QY of G-IL-CDs. Firstly, the G-IL-CDs solution and the quinine sulfate standard solution (in 0.1 M sulfuric acid) were placed in the analyzer. Their emission spectra were recorded after excitation, and the fluorescence intensities and absorbances were compared to obtain the QY of G-IL-CDs by the analyzer.

As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the G-IL-CDs exhibited uniform spherical structure with an average diameter of 2.30 ± 0.46 nm. The size distribution was concentrated within the range of 1.0–3.5 nm, displaying a trend of normal distribution. HR-TEM images of G-IL-CDs revealed multiple lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.18 nm. The XRD pattern of G-IL-CDs exhibited a broad and intense diffraction peak at 2θ = 20°, corresponding to the (002) graphite plane structure (Fig. 1C) [27]. The FT-IR spectra depicted a peak at 2930 cm−1, which was attributed to the stretching vibration of CH bonds (Fig. 1D) [28]. The broad peak at 1620 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO bonds [29]. The peaks at 1270 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of CF and CO bonds, respectively [30].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the electronic structure and surface elemental composition of G-IL-CDs. Five peaks at 286 eV, 402 eV, 533 eV, 686 eV, and 194 eV were exhibited in the XPS spectrum, which corresponded to the elements of C 1s (55.66 %), N 1s (12.03 %), O 1s (8.06 %), F 1s (16.41 %), and B 1s (7.84 %), respectively (Fig. S2A). The C 1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.4 eV, and 289.0 eV, which were attributed to the CC, CN, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S2B) [31]. The N 1s spectrum displayed two peaks at 399.1 eV and 401.7 eV, representing the CN and NH structures (Fig. S2C) [32]. The O 1s signal could be described by two peaks at 532.0 and 533.4 eV, which implied the presence of CO and CO bonds (Fig. S2D) [33]. In the F 1s spectrum, two different peaks corresponding to semi-ionic and covalent CF bonds appeared at 685.3 eV and 685.8 eV, respectively (Fig. S2E). Additionally, the B 1s spectrum illustrated characteristic peaks at 193.5 eV and 194.0 eV, which corresponded to the BC and BO bonds (Fig. S2F) [34]. The XPS analysis was consistent with the FT-IR results, indicating the successful doping of N, F, and B atoms into G-IL-CD, which contributed to enhancing the photoluminescence of G-IL-CDs.

The fluorescence and UV–visible absorption spectra were measured to explore the optical properties of G-IL-CDs. A strong absorption peak at 245 nm from the UV–visible absorption spectrum was observed, which was ascribed to the π → π* transition in the aromatic structure of the carbon core (Fig. 2A) [35]. Additionally, the peak at 278 nm further indicated the presence of non-conjugated n-electron moieties within the CDs. The G-IL-CDs solution exhibited a transparent light yellow under daylight, but it emitted bright green fluorescence under UV light. And the G-IL-CDs exhibited a maximum emission at 517 nm when the excitation wavelength was 440 nm (Fig. 2B). Moreover, there was a distinct dependence of the emission wavelength on the excitation wavelength, which was attributed to the surface defects within the G-IL-CDs. XPS data indicated that G-IL-CDs were doped with elements N, F, and B, and the surface contained various functional groups such as CC, CN, CO, NH, and CO. The introduction of these functional groups and the doping of elements could alter the electronic structure of G-IL-CDs, leading to changes in local electron density and the formation of surface defects. Transient fluorescence spectroscopy measurements revealed a QY of 20.86 % for G-IL-CDs, with an intensity-weighted average fluorescence lifetime of 1.2 ns (Fig. S3).

To ensure the accuracy and reliability of G-IL-CDs in practical applications, an investigation into their fluorescence stability was conducted. Each experiment was duplicated three times validate its outcome. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of G-IL-CDs solutions at different pH levels was examined. As the pH increased from 1.0 to 7.0, the fluorescence intensity gradually rose and reached its peak at pH 7.0. However, the fluorescence intensity rapidly declined with the further increase of pH. This was attributed to the protonation of functional groups on the surface of G-IL-CDs under acidic conditions and the deprotonation under alkaline conditions, both of which resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of G-IL-CDs. Notably, the G-IL-CDs could maintain relatively stable fluorescence intensity even under strong acidic conditions. Additionally, the impact of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of G-IL-CDs was studied by adding various concentrations of NaCl solution ranging from 1.0 × 10−7 to 1.0 × 10−1 M into 3 mL of G-IL-CDs solution. Even at NaCl concentration of 1.0 × 10−1 M, the CDs exhibited sustained high fluorescence intensity (Fig. S5). The fluorescence intensity was monitored at intervals of 1 min under continuous exposure to ultraviolet light. As shown in Fig. S6, after continuous exposure for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity of the G-IL-CDs solution remained remarkably stable. Finally, the effect of high temperature on the fluorescence intensity of G-IL-CDs was examined, and the results demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of G-IL-CDs was nearly unaffected as the temperature increased from 20 °C to 80 °C (Fig. S7). These experimental findings illustrated that the prepared G-IL-CDs solution exhibited excellent performance including good pH stability, salt resistance, high temperature tolerance, and resistance to photobleaching, which broadened its vast potential in the field of pepsin analysis.

Due to the difficulty and instability of directly detecting pepsin, the “increase-decrease” detection mechanism for measuring pepsin was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity at 517 nm notably increased after adding WP to G-IL-CDs solution. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity significantly decreased after 20 min of introducing pepsin into the solution. The relationship between the degree of fluorescence quenching (∆F) and the concentration of pepsin could be used to calculate the amount of pepsin in the actual sample because pepsin could hydrolyze WP and disrupt the interaction between WP and G-IL-CDs. To explain the mechanism for pepsin detection, it was necessary to investigate the interaction between WP and G-IL-CDs. Scanning electron microscopy results revealed a significant aggregation of G-IL-CDs in the presence of WP and the average nanoparticle size increased to 1.2 μm, indicating G-IL-CDs experienced aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement (Fig. S8). The introduction of pepsin for hydrolysis disrupted the relationship between WP and G-IL-CDs, restoring them to their original fluorescence state (Fig. S9).

To investigate interactions between WP and G-IL-CDs, the subsequent experiments were conducted using WP as the fluorophore and G-IL-CDs as the quencher. If the quenching mechanism is static quenching mechanism, it would indicate the formation of a static complex between WP and G-IL-CDs, leading to their binding. Since the Stern-Volmer equation could distinguish between static and dynamic quenching mechanisms, it was employed to validate this hypothesis [36]. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence spectrum of WP displayed strong fluorescence emission at 340 nm under excitation at 280 nm. The fluorescence intensity of WP gradually decreased with an increase in G-IL-CDs concentration, suggesting a concentration-dependent quenching of WP fluorescence by G-IL-CDs. Fig. 3C demonstrated that the fluorescence lifetime of WP was almost identical in the presence or absence of G-IL-CDs, suggesting that G-IL-CDs might quench the fluorescence of WP through static quenching. The fluorescence quenching constant for the G-IL-CDs-WP system could be calculated from the following Stern-Volmer (SV) equation:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K sv
Q
F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of WP and F denoted the fluorescence intensity of WP after the addition of G-IL-CDs. Ksv stood for the quenching constant of the G-IL-CDs-WP system, and [Q] represented the concentration of G-IL-CDs. The Ksv values for the G-IL-CDs-WP system decreased from 2.53 × 104 to 1.85 × 104 L/mol as the temperature increased, further indicating that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was deemed static (Fig. 3D). Additionally, the absorption spectrum of WP altered after the addition of G-IL-CDs, indicating the formation of a new complex between G-IL-CDs and WP (Fig. 3E).

The Double Logarithm equation is commonly used to analyze binding data in ligand-receptor binding experiments. Therefore, the Double Logarithm equation was used for calculating the binding constant Ka and the number of binding sites n in the G-IL-CDs-WP system:
(2)
lg
F 0
− F
F
= lg
K a
+ nlg
Q
F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of WP before and after the addition of G-IL-CDs, respectively. [Q] signified the concentration of G-IL-CDs in the mixed solution. Based on the measured fluorescence quenching spectra data, lg(F0 F)/F was plotted against lg[G-IL-CDs] at different temperatures (Fig. S10). The intercepts and slopes of the curves were used to calculate the binding constants Ka and n for the interaction between G-IL-CDs and WP (Table S1). Based on the parameter n ≈ 1 in the table, it could be inferred that G-IL-CDs had a binding site on WP. Moreover, the Ka values falling within the order of 104 suggested a substantial binding affinity between G-IL-CDs and WP.

The type of binding force between WP and G-IL-CDs could be determined by the thermodynamic parameters of the system, specifically the enthalpy change (ΔH) and entropy change (ΔS). The nature and magnitude of ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG could be calculated using the Van't Hoff equation as follows [37]:
(3)
ln
K a
= − ∆ H / TR + ∆ S / R
(4)
∆ G = ∆ H − T ∆ S

The equation stated that Ka represented the binding constant of the system at the respective temperature, where R was the gas constant, and T was the thermodynamic temperature. The values of ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG were calculated using Eqs. (3) and (4) (Table S1). According to Ross and Subramanian theory, the values of ΔH and ΔS suggested that hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces played a predominant role in the binding process between WP and G-IL-CDs [38]. Due to the abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the prepared G-IL-CDs, hydrogen bonding could readily form between WP and G-IL-CDs.

Fig. 3F illustrated the circular dichroism spectra of WP before and after the addition of G-IL-CDs. The spectra of WP displayed two distinct absorption bands at 210 nm and 218 nm, which were characteristic peaks associated with protein α-helix structures. It could be observed from the figure that after the addition of G-IL-CDs, there was a significant change in the ellipticity of WP, and the α-helix content of the protein increased from 43.9 % to 52.0 %. This indicated that the interaction between G-IL-CDs and WP led to alterations in secondary structure of WP.

The effects of pH, WP amount, incubation time, and incubation temperature on achieving high-sensitivity detection of pepsin within the system were investigated. Each test was duplicated and the experiment was independently repeated three times. Following the addition of pepsin, the fluorescence quenching value (ΔF) of the G-IL-CDs-WP system exhibited an initial increase followed by a decrease within the pH range of 1.0 to 5.0. The maximum pepsin activity was observed at pH 2.0 with minimal ΔF at pH 1.0, indicating the inactivation of pepsin in highly acidic solutions (Fig. 4A). Consequently, pH 2.0 was chosen as the condition for subsequent testing. Spectral data revealed that the maximum ΔF was observed at a WP concentration of 40 μL (0.1 g/mL) (Fig. 4B). The ΔF increased with enzymatic reaction time and reached stable after 20 min (Fig. 4C), indicating complete hydrolysis of WP by the pepsin at this point. In the optimization experiment for incubation temperature, the most significant fluorescence quenching was observed at 37 °C, and excessively high and low temperatures inhibited the activity of pepsin (Fig. 4D). Therefore, pH 2.0, 40 μL, 37 °C and 20 min was determined as the optimal incubation time for the enzymatic reaction.

Various interference substances were tested to verify the selectivity of the G-IL-CDs-WP system towards pepsin. When different interference ions (Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+) and interfering enzymes (Lip, Amy, Try) were added to the G-IL-CDs-WP system at identical concentrations, there was minimal change in the fluorescence quenching after a 20 min incubation period. However, the fluorescence quenching of G-IL-CDs-WP significantly increased in the presence of pepsin (Fig. 5A). This illustrated the excellent specificity of the G-IL-CDs-WP system in detecting pepsin in saliva. Under the optimized conditions, the sensitivity of G-IL-CDs-WP towards pepsin was evaluated. As the concentration of pepsin gradually increased, the fluorescence intensity of the G-IL-CDs-WP system progressively decreased. A good linear relationship between the ΔF and pepsin concentration was observed within the range of 1.0 × 10−6–1.0 × 10−1 g/mL (Fig. 5B). The linear equation was formulated as ΔF = 29.73 + 4.35lg[C] with R2 = 0.9999 and P = 3.11 × 10−9. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.081 μg/mL on the basis of LOD = 3σ/k, where σ denoted the standard deviation of 11 solutions without pepsin and k represented the slope of the linear equation. These results demonstrated that the method exhibited high sensitivity and a broad detection range, making it suitable for detecting pepsin in saliva due to the concentration of pepsin in GERD patients in the range of 0.003–22 μg/mL. Additionally, in comparison with other fluorescent probes for pepsin (Table S2), the G-IL-CDs-WP system exhibited a wider linear range, a lower detection limit, and faster response time. However, although the strongly acidic conditions of this method were more suitable for detecting pepsin, there were still some limitations under neutral or alkaline conditions that could affect the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection.

To verify the feasibility and accuracy of the G-IL-CDs-WP probe in practical applications, the detection of pepsin in actual samples was studied. Saliva samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant was collected and diluted tenfold with a buffer solution. The results of experiments indicated the recovery of pepsin ranging between 99.99 % and 103.67 % (Table S3). Three parallel measurements were conducted, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were below 3.0 %, indicating that the experiment had good reproducibility. Additionally, a t-test comparing this method with the HPLC method exhibited no significant difference between the two at a 95 % confidence level (Table S4). These results confirmed the successful application of the G-IL-CDs-WP fluorescent probe for detecting pepsin in saliva samples.

In summary, a novel biosensor was developed for the detection of pepsin based on the interaction between G-IL-CDs and WPs. The fluorescence intensity of G-IL-CDs increased significantly due to aggregation and non-radiative energy transfer. At pH 2.0, WP was hydrolyzed by pepsin, and the aggregation structure was disrupted and fluorescence intensity decreased. This strategy successfully achieved the specific detection of pepsin in saliva through the fluorescence changes of the G-IL-CDs-WP system. In comparison with other fluorescent probes for pepsin, the G-IL-CDs-WP system exhibited a wider linear range, a lower detection limit, and a faster response time. This research not only holds promise for clinical screening in GERD disease patients but also offers new avenues for developing CDs-based biosensors.

Chenzhao Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuhao Jiang: Validation, Methodology. Zhiyu Wu: Methodology, Conceptualization. Xiujuan Wang: Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jiawei Yang: Validation, Methodology. Junxian Li: Validation, Methodology. Shanshan Wei: Validation, Methodology. Guoying Sun: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhibing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This wok was financially supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation (20220101350JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.136665.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Update on the epidemiology of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease: a systematic review
Gut
